phrasal_verbs.meaning: stop demanding sth, stop saying that you will do sth
give to a person in authority
introduce a new law or system
give little or no punishment; make a bomb, etc explode
follow sb/sth quickly in order to catch them
explode; be fired (for a gun, usually accidentally)
trick sb into believing sth that is not true

permission: have/ask for/get permission (from sb) to do
solution: have/find/think of/work out/come up with/figure out a solution (to sth)
threaten: to do, threaten sb with sth

A.title: Topic vocabulary in contrast
A.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words in the box.

questions.sentence: All twelve members of the ...... listened carefully to the defence lawyer's closing argument.
Most people can't imagine why anyone would ...... such a violent crime.
The new head teacher said the school ...... would be strictly enforced from the beginning of term.
The prosecution called a forensic scientist as their key ...... on the second day of the trial.
Many countries have banned ...... punishment in schools, arguing that it causes psychological harm.
Citizens in a democracy have the ...... to protest peacefully against government policies.
Those who ...... the rules of the road put other drivers and pedestrians at serious risk.
Some politicians argue that existing ...... need to be updated to deal with online crime.
The defendant had already been ...... twice before for similar offences.
The family of the victim stated that ...... had finally been served after many years of waiting.
A number of countries still use ...... punishment, despite growing international opposition.
After a three-week trial, the defendant was ...... to eight years in prison.
Several ...... captured the incident on their mobile phones and shared it online.
The ...... reminded the jury that they must reach their verdict based solely on the evidence.

B.title: Crossword
B.instructions: Solve the crossword using the clues below.

across.clue: A person who causes disturbance and trouble in public, especially at sports events. (8)
The formal decision reached by a judge or jury at the end of a trial. (7)
Someone who takes things belonging to others without permission. (5)
Information or objects used in a court of law to help prove whether something is true. (8)
A person who the police believe may be responsible for a crime. (7)
The jury found her not ... of the charges against her. (6)
To officially accuse someone of a crime and take legal action against them in court. (9)

down.clue: A professional who is qualified to advise on legal matters and represent people in court. (6)
To take someone into police custody when you believe they have committed a crime. (6)
A person who deliberately damages or destroys public or private property. (6)
If you are not guilty of a crime, you are described as this. (8)
To formally accuse someone of committing a crime so they must appear in court. (6)
To treat a person or group cruelly and unfairly, often because of their beliefs or identity. (9)
The person who is on trial in a court of law, also known as the defendant. (7)

C.title: Phrasal verbs (gap fill)
C.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the phrasal verbs in the box.

questions.sentence: Members of the public who witnessed the accident were asked to ...... and give their accounts to investigators.
The two suspects ...... through a back alley before the police arrived.
Inspectors are ...... reports of unsafe working conditions at the factory.
The council is ...... a new policy to tackle antisocial behaviour in the town centre.
A masked man ...... the post office and demanded all the cash in the till.
Two high-risk prisoners ...... of the detention centre by hiding in a delivery van.
The security guard ...... the shoplifter, eventually catching him at the car park exit.
Fortunately, the fire alarm ...... just in time for everyone to evacuate the building safely.

D.title: Phrasal verbs (gap fill)
D.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
D.passage: The strangest thing happened to me this morning. I was leaving the gym when I noticed a sports bag near the exit that looked exactly like mine. I picked it up without checking and was heading out to (1) ...... it in at reception, just to make sure, when a gym instructor ran up and told me I was stealing. I tried to explain the situation but she wouldn't (2) ...... down. 'I won't let you get (3) ...... with this,' she said. 'CCTV is everywhere and you'll be caught.' She blocked my path and started to take (4) ...... my name and membership number. I begged her to (5) ...... me off and explained I'd simply made a mistake. Suddenly my cousin Elena and a TV presenter burst through the doors laughing. The whole gym had been in on it, and I'd been completely (6) ...... in!

questions.sentence: I don't know why you keep **putting / taking** the blame on your teammates.
My dad has no **intention / purpose** of giving up his morning run, no matter how cold it is.
It **doesn't / isn't** necessary to book a table at that café – they always have space.
The manager **made / gave** us permission to leave early and attend the concert.
We don't know who was at **fault / damage** for the mix-up, but it needs to be resolved.
Sorry, I mistook her **for / with** someone I went to school with.
Should coaches take a player's age into **reason / account** when planning their training schedule?
Everything **went / had** wrong when the navigation app crashed in the middle of the drive.
We need clear traffic signs in **solution / order** to keep the roads safe for everyone.
A number of tourists are **making / taking** advantage of the new discount scheme.
They accused me **I** of damaging the car.
The cyclist was arrested **A** for running a red light.
Three people have been charged **H** with vandalism.
The student denied **E** cheating in the exam.
I doubt whether **B** the witness is reliable.
I caught a glimpse **D** of someone breaking into the building.
Peer pressure made me **G** join in with the bullying.
She says she was made **F** to hand over her wallet.
It is illegal for **C** anyone to drive without a valid licence.

G.title: Cloze (Word patterns)
G.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
G.passage: Marco Bianchi used to be my hero. One of the most celebrated chefs of his generation, Marco made us believe that great food could also be ethical. I had so much respect (1) ...... him, particularly because of his 'farm to table' philosophy and his vocal campaign for sustainable sourcing. And now there's no doubt that all the time Bianchi was claiming (2) ...... his ingredients were locally grown, he was importing cheap produce from overseas. Last month, a food standards investigation found Bianchi guilty (3) ...... misleading consumers on a large scale. Bianchi has been banned from using certain quality labels for the next three years, and the agency is threatening (4) ...... take similar cases to court in future. I hope they do. I refuse (5) ...... accept that we should feel sympathy for Bianchi at a time like this. We should never forgive people like Marco Bianchi (6) ...... abusing the trust of their customers.

H.title: Word formation (error correction)
H.instructions: Each sentence contains an error. The incorrect word is shown in bold. Write the correct form on the line.

questions.sentence: Many people question whether sending young ...... (offence) to a detention centre actually reduces future crime.
The defence insisted there was absolutely no solid ...... (prove) that their client had even been in the building.
'I won't answer any more questions until I've been able to speak to my ...... (law),' the man said firmly.
You really shouldn't make such serious ...... (accuse) unless you have something concrete to support them.
After retiring from the force, she spent several years working as a private ...... (investigate).
'I hope that your period of ...... (prison) has given you time to reflect on your actions,' the judge said.
Due to the nature of the offences, she was moved to a maximum ...... (secure) facility.
Deception and ...... (honest) are at the heart of many white-collar crimes.
Detectives are analysing all the forensic ...... (evident) collected from the scene.
Experts confirmed that the signature on the document was a ...... (forge).
The court heard that ...... (addict) had driven the defendant to steal in order to fund his habit.
The ...... (rob) was captured on a security camera outside the shop.
Anyone can become a ...... (theft) given the right circumstances, according to the psychologist.
The ...... (convict) of the former company director made headlines across the country.
Critics argue that overcrowded prisons are full of experienced ...... (crime) who share techniques with first-time offenders.
The question of whether a ...... (murder) should receive a life sentence remains a controversial one.
